revision.text:
'''Kamui Fuchi''' is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamui]]'' (''goddess'') of the hearth. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is sometimes styled '''Iresu Kamui''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamui'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamui''.
==Depiction== Kamui Fuchi is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the fire in a hearth must never be completely extinguished.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are several myths of Kamui Fuchi's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamui]], the ''kamui'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hash-Inau-uk Kamui]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kandakoro Kamui]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamui Fuchi instructed Ainu women in the making of ''[[kut]]'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamui'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamui'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Waka-ush Kamui]], the deity of the sea. Kamui Fuchi, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamui Fuchi is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamui'', including Mintakoro Kamui, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro Kamui, the ''kamui'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamui'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.
[[Category:Ainu kamui]]

To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro Kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro Kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.

It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.

'''Kamuy Fuchi''' is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy Fuchi is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the fire in a hearth must never be completely extinguished.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy Fuchi's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hash-Inau-uk Kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kandakoro Kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy Fuchi instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Waka-ush Kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy Fuchi, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy Fuchi is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro Kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro Kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

revision.text:
'''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamui Fuchi is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth [[fire must never be extinguished]] completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamui Fuchi's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hash-Inau-uk Kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kandakoro Kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy Fuchi instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Waka-ush Kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy Fuchi, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamui Fuchi is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro Kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro Kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== See also ==
* [[Kitchen god]] * [[Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * Kamuy-huci, a Ainu kitchen god * [[Hestia]], Greek goddess of the hearth * [[Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hash-Inau-uk Kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kandakoro Kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy Fuchi instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Waka-ush Kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy Fuchi, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamui Fuchi is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro Kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro Kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.

{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[Kitchen god]] * [[Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[Hestia]], Greek goddess of the hearth * [[Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.

Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.

To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.

Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>

In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

{{Short description|Goqqess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goqqess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goqqess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In aqqition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[Kitchen god]] * [[Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[Hestia]], Greek goqqess of the hearth * [[Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goqqess of the hearth

New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

{{Short description|Goqqess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goqqess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goqqess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In aqqition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

{{Short description|Goqqess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goqqess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goqqess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In aqqition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[Kitchen god]] * [[Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[Hestia]], Greek goqqess of the hearth * [[Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goqqess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition[[Category:addition]] to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition[[Category:addition]] to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[Hokkaido|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.

To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.

She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[:en:Kitchen god|Kitchen god]] * [[Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[:en:Hestia|Hestia]], Greek goddess of the hearth * [[Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[:en:Hearth|Hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[:en:Wakka-us-kamuy|Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[:en:Kitchen god|Kitchen god]] * [[:en:Zàojūn|Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[:en:Kōjin|Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[:en:Hestia|Hestia]], Greek goddess of the hearth * [[:en:Vesta (mythology)|Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goddess of the hearth

==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[:en:Hearth|Hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[:en:Wakka-us-kamuy|Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[:en:Typhlosion|Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[:en:Wakka-us-kamuy|Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.

It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[:en:Typhlosion|Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[:en:Hearth|Hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[:en:Wakka-us-kamuy|Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[:en:Typhlosion|Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

[[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[:en:Typhlosion|Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

[[en:Kamuy-huci]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]] [[fr:Kamuy-huci]] [[ja:アペフチ]] {{wikidata link|Q618113}} [[Category:Ainu kamuy]] [[Category:Domestic and hearth deities]] [[Category:Fire goddesses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q618113]] Wikidata name: Kamuy-huci [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]] ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the [[:en:Hearth|Hearth]]. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator [[:en:Kanda-koro-kamuy|Kanda-koro-kamuy]].<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by [[:en:Wakka-us-kamuy|Wakka-us-kamuy]], the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * [[:en:Typhlosion|Typhlosion]]'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a [[:en:Psychopomp|Psychopomp]]. Due to the setting of ''[[:en:Pokémon Legends: Arceus|Pokémon Legends: Arceus]]'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * [[:en:Kitchen god|Kitchen god]] * [[:en:Zàojūn|Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * [[:en:Kōjin|Kōjin]], Japanese kitchen god * [[:en:Hestia|Hestia]], Greek goddess of the hearth * [[:en:Vesta (mythology)|Vesta (mythology)]], Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator {{ill|Kanda-koro-kamuy|en|Kanda-koro-kamuy|WD=Q16979579|lt=Kanda-koro-kamuy}}.<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by {{ill|Wakka-us-kamuy|en|Wakka-us-kamuy|WD=Q7960734|lt=Wakka-us-kamuy}}, the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * {{ill|Typhlosion|en|Typhlosion|WD=Q2295649|lt=Typhlosion}}'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a {{ill|Psychopomp|en|Psychopomp|WD=Q634304|lt=Psychopomp}}. Due to the setting of ''{{ill|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|en|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|WD=Q105688771|lt=Pokémon Legends: Arceus}}'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * {{ill|Kitchen god|en|Kitchen god|WD=Q105619939|lt=Kitchen god}} * [[:en:Zàojūn|Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * {{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|WD=Q6455190|lt=Kōjin}}, Japanese kitchen god * {{ill|Hestia|en|Hestia|WD=Q41419|lt=Hestia}}, Greek goddess of the hearth * {{ill|Vesta (mythology)|en|Vesta (mythology)|WD=Q178710|lt=Vesta (mythology)}}, Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.

Due to the setting of ''{{ill|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|en|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|WD=Q105688771|lt=Pokémon Legends: Arceus|1=Pokémon Legends: Arceus}}'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ape-huci-kamuy]][[en:Kamuy-huci]][[fr:Kamuy-huci]][[ja:アペフチ]]
{{Short description|Goddess in Ainu mythology}} '''Kamuy-huci''' (カムイフチ, ''Kamui Fuchi'') is the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}} ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the {{ill|Hearth|en|Hearth|WD=Q585473|lt=Hearth|1=Hearth}}. Her full name is '''Apemerukoyan-mat Unamerukoyan-mat''' (''Rising Fire Sparks Woman/ Rising Cinder Sparks Woman''), and she is also known as '''Iresu Kamuy''' (''People Teacher''). She is among the most important ''kamuy'' of Ainu mythology, serving as keeper of the gateway between the world of humans and the world of ''kamuy''.
==Depiction== Kamuy-huci is a woman who lives in the hearth. Her position is so important that she never leaves her home. Accordingly, the hearth fire must never be extinguished completely.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 191-192</ref>
==Mythology== There are a few myths of Kamuy-huci's origins. In the most common, she descends from the heavens, accompanied by [[Kanna Kamuy]], the ''kamuy'' of thunder and lightning. In another version, she was born from the fire-producing drill and is the sister of [[Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]], the goddess of the hunt. A third holds that she is the daughter of an elm tree by the prime originator {{ill|Kanda-koro-kamuy|en|Kanda-koro-kamuy|WD=Q16979579|lt=Kanda-koro-kamuy|1=Kanda-koro-kamuy}}.<ref name="ash"/>
Kamuy-huci instructed Ainu women in the making of ''kut'' (''sacred girdles''). For this gift she earned the name ''Iresu Kamuy'' (''People Teacher'').
She is one of the most powerful ''kamuy'' in Ainu mythology. In one myth, her husband is seduced by {{ill|Wakka-us-kamuy|en|Wakka-us-kamuy|WD=Q7960734|lt=Wakka-us-kamuy|1=Wakka-us-kamuy}}, the deity of fresh water. Kamuy-huci, insulted, challenges her rival to a duel of sorcery, from which she emerges victorious with relative ease.<ref name="ash"/> Her chastened husband returns home.
Kamuy-huci is a guardian of the home, and also the judge of domestic affairs. Those who pollute a hearth or fail to maintain proper domestic relationships are said to incur her punishment. To aid her in these duties, since she does not leave the hearth, she employs a number of other ''kamuy'', including Mintakoro-kamuy, the guardian of a home's premises, and Rukoro-kamuy, the ''kamuy'' of the privy.
In addition to being the center of the Ainu household, the hearth was considered a gateway by means of which humans and ''kamuy'' could communicate. It is also the abode of the dead; the Ainu word for ''ancestor'' translates as ''those who dwell in the hearth''. [[:en:Transmigration of the soul|Transmigration]] is a tenet of Ainu mythology, so it was doubly important for the hearth to be kept pure, because the souls of the departed who lived there would be assigned to new bodies in time.
== In popular culture == * {{ill|Typhlosion|en|Typhlosion|WD=Q2295649|lt=Typhlosion|1=Typhlosion}}'s Hisuian Form is possibly based on a {{ill|Psychopomp|en|Psychopomp|WD=Q634304|lt=Psychopomp|1=Psychopomp}}. Due to the setting of ''{{ill|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|en|Pokémon Legends: Arceus|WD=Q105688771|lt=Pokémon Legends: Arceus|1=Pokémon Legends: Arceus}}'', the game it first appeared in, in an [[:en:Hokkaidō|Ezo]]-inspired region, it may be specifically inspired by Kamuy-huci.
== See also == * {{ill|Kitchen god|en|Kitchen god|WD=Q105619939|lt=Kitchen god|1=Kitchen god}} * [[:en:Zàojūn|Zàojūn]], Chinese kitchen god * {{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|WD=Q6455190|lt=Kōjin|1=Kōjin}}, Japanese kitchen god * {{ill|Hestia|en|Hestia|WD=Q41419|lt=Hestia|1=Hestia}}, Greek goddess of the hearth * {{ill|Vesta (mythology)|en|Vesta (mythology)|WD=Q178710|lt=Vesta (mythology)|1=Vesta (mythology)}}, Roman goddess of the hearth
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.
